bank: Headlines with stronger emotional words generate more clicks than those with neutral language.
Headlines using numbers or lists attract more clicks than those without numerical elements.
Headlines phrased as questions receive more clicks than declarative headlines.
Headlines that imply exclusivity or urgency get more clicks than those that do not.
Headlines containing popular buzzwords or trending terms outperform more generic headlines.
Shorter headlines (under 10 words) attract more clicks than longer, more complex ones.
Headlines that include the promise of a solution or benefit generate more clicks than purely informational headlines.
Headlines using active voice get more clicks than those using passive voice.
Headlines with personal pronouns ('you', 'your') appeal more and get more clicks than impersonal headlines.
Headlines that create curiosity or suspense receive more clicks than straightforward, descriptive headlines.
Headlines with clear, specific topics outperform vague or ambiguous headlines.
Headlines that highlight well-known celebrities or brands get more clicks than those that do not mention popular entities.
Headlines using superlatives ('best', 'worst', 'most') attract more clicks than neutral comparatives.
Headlines with proper capitalization and punctuation get more clicks than those with irregular formatting.
Headlines that avoid clickbait exaggeration tend to sustain more clicks than overly sensational headlines.
